content:
# Deuteronomy Chapter 20 — The EMET Bible (Literal)

**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 110v–110r | **Source Images:** DEU_020_page_001.jpg, DEU_020_page_002.jpg | **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 155–156 | **LXX Images:** DEU_020_vaticanus_page_001.jpg, DEU_020_vaticanus_page_002.jpg

---

**1** When you go out to battle against your enemies and see horses and chariots, a people more numerous than you, you shall not be afraid of them, for the LORD your God is with you, who brought you up from the land of Egypt.

**2** And it shall be, when you draw near to the battle, that the priest shall approach and speak to the people,

**3** and shall say to them: Hear, O Yisra'el! You are drawing near today to battle against your enemies. Do not let your heart grow faint; do not be afraid, and do not panic, and do not be terrified before them,

**4** for the LORD your God is he who goes with you to fight for you against your enemies, to save you.

**5** And the officers shall speak to the people, saying: Who is the man who has built a new house and has not dedicated it? Let him go and return to his house, lest he die in the battle and another man dedicate it.

**6** And who is the man who has planted a vineyard and has not yet made common use of it? Let him go and return to his house, lest he die in the battle and another man make common use of it.

**7** And who is the man who has betrothed a woman and has not taken her? Let him go and return to his house, lest he die in the battle and another man take her.

**8** And the officers shall continue speaking to the people, and they shall say: Who is the man who is fearful and faint of heart? Let him go and return to his house, lest the heart of his brothers melt like his heart.

**9** And it shall be, when the officers finish speaking to the people, that they shall appoint commanders of the armies at the head of the people.

**10** When you draw near to a city to fight against it, you shall call out to it [terms of] peace.

**11** And it shall be, if it answers you with peace and opens to you, then all the people found in it shall be [subject] to you as forced labor and shall serve you.

**12** But if it does not make peace with you, but makes war against you, then you shall besiege it.

**13** And when the LORD your God gives it into your hand, you shall strike every male in it with the edge of the sword.

**14** Only the women and the little ones and the livestock and everything that is in the city, all its spoil, you shall plunder for yourself; and you shall consume the spoil of your enemies that the LORD your God has given to you.

**15** Thus you shall do to all the cities that are very far from you, which are not of the cities of these nations.

**16** But from the cities of these peoples that the LORD your God is giving you as an inheritance, you shall not let any soul live.

**17** For you shall utterly devote them to destruction—the Hittite and the Amorite, the Canaanite and the Perizzite, the Hivite and the Jebusite—as the LORD your God has commanded you,

**18** so that they may not teach you to do according to all their abominations that they have done for their gods, and so you would sin against the LORD your God.

**19** When you besiege a city for many days to fight against it to capture it, you shall not destroy its trees by swinging an axe against them; for from them you may eat, and you shall not cut them down. For is the tree of the field a man, that it should come against you in the siege?

**20** Only the tree that you know is not a food tree—it you may destroy and cut down; and you shall build siege-works against the city that makes war with you until it falls.

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

**v.1 "horses and chariots"** — Hebrew *susim ve-rekhev*, a hendiadys for chariot-borne cavalry forces, the overwhelming military technology of the ancient Near East. EMET preserves both terms.

**v.3 "panic"** — Hebrew *ta'arots*, from *arats* ("to tremble/dread"). Four verbs of fear are listed in sequence: *yirak levavkhem / tira'u / tachpazu / tir'atsu*. EMET renders each distinctly: "grow faint / be afraid / panic / be terrified."

**v.5–7 "make common use of"** — Hebrew *chilelo* (from *chalal*, Pi'el), referring to the first-use profanation rite when a vineyard's fruit becomes permissible after four years (cf. Lev 19:23–25). EMET uses "make common use of" rather than "enjoy its fruit" to flag the legal-ritual dimension.

**v.10 "[terms of] peace"** — Hebrew *leshalom*, lit. "to peace/shalom." The bracketed gloss clarifies that a proclamation is meant, not simply a greeting.

**v.16 "any soul"** — Hebrew *kol neshama*, lit. "every breath/soul." EMET preserves this stark totality-formula.

**v.19 "lo tashchit"** — The phrase *lo tashchit* ("you shall not destroy") gives rise to the rabbinic principle of the same name prohibiting wanton destruction. EMET translates literally: "you shall not destroy its trees." The rhetorical question *ki ha-adam ets ha-sadeh* ("for is the tree of the field a man?") is rendered as a direct question. LXX Vaticanus (p. 155–156) reads *me anthropos to dendron to en to agro* — also a question, consistent with MT.

**v.20 "siege-works"** — Hebrew *matsod* (or *metsorah*), denoting earthen siege ramps or circumvallation structures. EMET renders with the general term "siege-works."

**LXX general note** — Vaticanus pp. 155–156 contains the full chapter without major additions or omissions. The MT reading *charema tachrimem* in v.17 (utterly devote to destruction) is matched by LXX *anathemati anathematieseis autous*, showing complete agreement on the herem formula.

*— EMET*
